Early Modern

Damaris Cudworth Masham

1659 – 1708

Moral agency, education, and the reflective life beyond Lockean orthodoxy.

I refer to Damaris Masham as “she” throughout: Lady Damaris Masham, 1658/9–1708, was one of the most philosophically important English women of the late seventeenth and early eighteenth centuries. Her philosophy of mind is not presented in one treatise called “on the soul” or “on the mind.” It must be reconstructed from three bodies of material: her two anonymous published works, A Discourse concerning the Love of God (1696) and Occasional Thoughts in Reference to a Vertuous or Christian Life (1705), plus her correspondence with Locke and Leibniz. The result is not a loose miscellany. It is a coherent, if unsystematically expressed, theistic, empiricist, rationalist, anti-occasionalist, morally practical philosophy of mind. Masham’s mind is not a Cartesian spectator-substance sealed off from the world. It is an embodied, affective, educable, socially situated, free, rational, God-related agent, whose cognitive and moral health depends on the right use of reason, the right government of desire, and the right orientation of love.

The most compressed thesis is this: Masham’s philosophy of mind treats mind as rational agency under the law of reason or nature, but it refuses to sever reason from love, pleasure, habit, embodiment, sociality, revelation, or the wider hierarchy of created intelligent beings. Her thought about God’s mind and angelic minds is not a detachable theological ornament. It is part of the architecture of the theory: God’s mind grounds intelligible order, moral law, and creaturely happiness; angels and other intelligent beings show that human mentality is one finite mode within a larger intellectual creation, not the measure of all mind.

1. Sources, reception, and the international frame

Masham’s two main works are religiously titled, but they address core philosophical topics: love, happiness, virtue, knowledge of God, free will, rational belief, education, and the relation between created beings and God. The Stanford Encyclopedia entry rightly stresses that her philosophy is shaped both by her father Ralph Cudworth’s Cambridge Platonism and by Locke’s empiricism, while her later correspondence with Leibniz addresses pre-established harmony, substance, and free will.

The non-Anglophone reception is especially useful here. The French reception began early: Pierre Coste translated A Discourse concerning the Love of God as Discours sur l’amour divin in Amsterdam in 1705; the Bibliothèque nationale de France catalogue identifies Masham as the author and Coste as translator. A recent French article, Damaris Masham face aux philosophes, explicitly warns that Coste’s translation gives the text a more Lockean accent, while also situating Masham in dialogue with Locke, Leibniz, Malebranche, Norris, Cudworth, Bayle, Astell, Le Clerc, Limborch, and the Republic of Letters. That is important: Masham should not be reduced either to “Locke’s disciple” or to “Cudworth’s daughter.” Her thought is an independent synthesis.

Spanish- and Portuguese-language scholarship also helps. Viridiana Platas’s Spanish article on the Masham-Leibniz correspondence analyzes it through ontological, epistemological, and critical dimensions, presenting Masham’s work as a combination of Platonism and empiricism that displays intellectual independence. A recent Italian venue, Lo Sguardo, frames the Masham-Leibniz exchange within the République des Lettres and emphasizes that Masham pressed Leibniz to give a more rigorous exposition of pre-established harmony while defending Cudworth’s system against Bayle-related criticisms; its bibliography also records Portuguese work on the Leibniz-Masham correspondence. These sources reinforce a methodological point: Masham’s philosophy of mind emerges not only from English moral theology, but from a European debate about mind, matter, causation, plastic nature, freedom, and divine order.

2. Terminology: “mind,” “soul,” “reason,” “understanding,” “passion”

Masham does not use “mind” in a narrow modern sense. Her vocabulary includes “mind,” “soul,” “understanding,” “reason,” “passions,” “appetites,” “affections,” “desires,” “love,” “inclinations,” “habits,” “will,” and “liberty of action.” A reconstructed philosophy of mind must therefore treat mind as a complex practical-cognitive whole. It includes cognition, affect, moral orientation, habit, freedom, and relation to God.

In A Discourse concerning the Love of God, love is explicitly defined as a “disposition” or “act of the mind” toward what pleases us. Masham denies Norris’s attempt to divide love into fundamentally different kinds, such as love of desire for God and love of benevolence for creatures. Love is one simple act of mind; desire and benevolence are further acts that follow from love according to the nature of the object loved. This is one of her most important pieces of philosophical psychology. It means that affect is not irrational residue. Love is a mental act; pleasure discloses objects as lovable; reason must regulate love without pretending that love can or should be abolished.

In Occasional Thoughts, virtue is described as the most active principle of the mind: a sincere desire to conform in all things to the law set by the Maker. That is a crucial formula. Virtue is not mere external conduct, reputation, chastity, social polish, or conformity to custom. It is an active mental principle: desire disciplined by reason and directed toward divine law.

Reason, for Masham, is the faculty by which we discover the connection among ideas through intermediate ideas; it judges whether assent is certain, probable, or unsupported. She also says that reason distinguishes human beings from beasts, and the actual use of reason distinguishes rational persons from madmen. This places her near Locke epistemologically, but she gives reason a strong moral and theological role closer to Cudworth: reason discovers not only empirical connections but the law of nature, the relations of things, and our duty as rational creatures.

3. The basic architecture: empiricism plus rational moral order

Masham’s mind is empiricist in origin but rationalist in norm. She accepts that our knowledge immediately received from sense or reflection is enlarged by reason. From self-existence, the order and beauty of the universe, and the pleasure we receive from life and objects around us, reason can infer a first cause that is intelligent, powerful, wise, and good. But this empiricism does not become skeptical naturalism. The world is morally and teleologically ordered, and the mind’s task is to discern the relations and dependencies among things.

This dual inheritance matters. If one emphasizes only Locke, Masham looks like a religious empiricist. If one emphasizes only Cudworth, she looks like a Platonist moral rationalist. The better reconstruction is that she is both: ideas and evidence arise through experience, but the relations among things disclose a law of reason or nature. That law is not invented by human beings, nor is it an arbitrary divine decree. It is bound to the nature of things and to the wisdom and goodness of God.

Her account of rational belief is especially strong. She insists that reason must guide assent, including assent to revelation. A proposition above our comprehension may be rationally believed if reason has established that it is genuinely revealed by God; but it is irrational to demand assent to something as revelation if reason does not attest that it is revealed, and still more irrational to demand assent to what is evidently contrary to reason. This is a philosophy of mind as epistemic responsibility. The mind is not pious because it suppresses judgment; it is pious when it uses the faculty God gave it.

This also explains her hostility to “enthusiasm,” superstition, sectarian irrationality, and merely traditional belief. Bad religious instruction teaches children to silence reason. It leaves them vulnerable to skepticism, atheism, or blind conformity. Masham’s philosophy of mind is therefore also a theory of intellectual formation: minds become rational or irrational through pedagogy, custom, habits of inquiry, and social permission to ask for reasons.

4. The psychology of love: pleasure, desire, benevolence, and God

Masham’s Discourse is indispensable for philosophy of mind because it gives a subtle theory of love. Against John Norris and Malebranchean occasionalism, she argues that love is not divided into a wholly divine desire-love and a merely creaturely benevolence-love. Love is a simple mental disposition toward what pleases us; different objects generate different subsequent acts. Toward thinking beings we love, we naturally have benevolence and desire to enjoy what delights us in them; toward inanimate things, love may be followed mainly by desire to enjoy them.

This makes Masham’s psychology anti-ascetic in a precise sense. She does not defend disordered appetite. She explicitly treats the right regulation of passion and appetite as central to virtue. But she rejects the view that love for creatures is intrinsically spiritually corrupt. Creaturely love is not a competitor to divine love unless it is inordinate, that is, unless it displaces love of God or neighbor.

The epistemological implication is striking: we learn to love God through the created world. God is invisible; we are led by his works to know and love him. Masham asks how anyone who never loved creatures could love God. The loveliness of creation gives us the idea and experience of love through which we come to love the Creator. This is a direct rejection of the Malebranchean-Norris view that God must be the sole object of desirous love because God alone is the true cause of our pleasures.

Thus Masham’s theory of love is also a theory of intentionality. Mental acts are directed toward objects experienced as pleasing, good, useful, beautiful, or lovable. The mind’s affective orientation is not sealed within itself; it opens onto the created world and, through the world, toward God. Love is relational cognition as well as desire.

5. Anti-occasionalism and the reality of creaturely mediation

Masham’s disagreement with Norris is not merely pastoral. It is metaphysical and psychological. Norris’s Malebranchean background treats God as the sole efficient cause of our sensations and pleasures, with creatures serving only as occasions. Masham thinks that even if such a system could be defended speculatively, it gives us no intelligible or morally useful basis for ordinary life. It makes creation too much like a set of ciphers and threatens to dissolve the practical reality of social affection.

Her criticism has several layers. First, she rejects speculative hypotheses that rest on what human beings cannot conceive rather than on evidence. Second, she argues that the observable world is not spiritually dispensable: creation mediates knowledge of God and the love of God. Third, she worries about moral consequences. If creatures are not genuine goods to us, then love of neighbor, friendship, family affection, and social cohesion lose their natural basis. She explicitly says that if we had no desire except toward God, human societies could not hold together and mankind could not continue.

This yields a broader principle: a viable philosophy of mind must preserve the functional and moral significance of creaturely relations. The mind is not built for solitary absorption into God in the present life. It is built for rational love of God and neighbor in an embodied social world.

6. Passion, appetite, and the government of reason

Masham’s mind is not divided into a pure rational faculty and a merely animal body. Passions and appetites are part of human mentality. They become morally dangerous not because they exist, but because they can govern judgment, form bad habits, or supply self-serving principles. She says many people use reason only for the gratification of passions; if they do not conform action to reason, they try to warp reason into compliance with their practices.

Her account of virtue is therefore regulatory rather than eliminative. The goal is not the extinction of desire but the subjection of appetite to right reason. She repeatedly connects virtue with the government of passions and appetites, and she emphasizes that early habits of self-denial and restraint must be established before mature rational argument alone is expected to do the work.

This is a sophisticated developmental psychology. Mature vice is not just a bad choice made by a fully formed adult. It is often the result of early malformation: wrong instruction, lack of rational education, bad habits, the social prestige of fashion, and the failure to cultivate the understanding. Once passions are strong and habits settled, reason may arrive too late or too weakly.

Masham’s view is therefore not narrowly intellectualist. She does not think that merely knowing the good automatically produces the good. The mind must be trained affectively and habitually so that reason can actually govern.

7. Education and the formation of minds

Masham’s arguments for women’s education are often treated as proto-feminist social criticism, which they are. But they are also central to her philosophy of mind. She argues that the improvement of the understanding through useful knowledge is necessary to the regulation of manners, and that neglecting women’s education harms the formation of children’s minds because mothers usually shape the earliest impressions, inclinations, and passions of both sexes.

The key claim is psychological: early impressions have a strong and often unalterable influence on future inclinations and passions. The “right forming of the Minds” of children must begin early; good habits and principles must be established before the mind is tainted by contrary influences. This makes education not merely the transmission of information but the architecture of agency. To educate is to form attention, desire, judgment, and emotional regulation.

Masham’s view of women’s intellectual capacity is also clear. She criticizes a social order in which women are discouraged from improving reason and then blamed for ignorance. She remarks that a woman with even a little philosophy may be suspected of atheism, and she explicitly attributes women’s lack of knowledge to social discouragement rather than natural incapacity.

This has direct consequences for philosophy of mind. Rational agency is not distributed by gender. The faculties of reason and moral understanding are common to rational creatures. What differs is cultivation. Minds are socially enabled or disabled.

8. Freedom, will, and moral accountability

Masham’s moral psychology depends on freedom. She describes human beings as endowed with a capacity to perceive differences among things and with liberty to act, or not act, suitably to them. We can act according to the preference of our own minds either in conformity or disconformity with the will of the Creator.

Her strongest formulation appears in Occasional Thoughts: without a capacity in the creature to act contrary to the will of the Creator, there could be no defect, desert, or self-excellence in created beings. Freedom or liberty of action is the greatest endowment a created being can have. This is not a casual remark. It defines the structure of moral agency. A mind is morally accountable because it can act otherwise than it ought, not merely because it can register moral propositions.

This is why she is wary of Leibniz’s pre-established harmony. In the correspondence, she worries that a system in which God has prearranged the correspondence of soul and body may threaten liberty or free agency. Lascano’s analysis highlights that Masham takes the inner feeling of being free, together with moral accountability before God, as strong evidence of freedom.

Masham is not giving a fully technical libertarian theory of the will in the later analytic sense. But she is committed to real freedom of action, moral accountability, and the creature’s ability to act contrary to God’s will. Her view is anti-fatalist and anti-determinist in moral intent.

9. Mind and body: against Cartesian unextended substance, occasionalism, and pre-established harmony

Masham’s published works do not offer a complete metaphysical treatise on the mind-body problem. The fullest evidence comes from her correspondence with Leibniz. The result is philosophically important and somewhat radical.

She objects to the notion of unextended substance. In the letter material quoted and analyzed by Lascano, Masham says that extension is inseparable from her notion of substance and that she has no conception of unextended substance. She does not merely say that unextended substance is hard to imagine; she treats the lack of any conception of it as a reason not to attribute properties to it.

Her positive suggestion is that all substance may be extended, but extension need not be solidity. She distinguishes extension without solidity from solid extension. She also entertains the possibility that God could annex the power of thinking to a solid substance, and that thought and solidity may be attributes of one unknown substance.

This position should be classified carefully. It is not reductive materialism. Masham does not argue that thought is mechanically produced by matter; indeed, she thinks thought cannot naturally be derived from extension or solidity alone. Nor is it straightforward Cartesian dualism, because she rejects the standard Cartesian notion of an unextended thinking substance. The best label is something like “theistic non-Cartesian extended-substance theory,” with openness to a Lockean “thinking matter” possibility and a Cambridge Platonist background of immaterial or spiritual extension.

This also explains her resistance to Leibniz. Pre-established harmony may be elegant and may even be consistent with divine wisdom, but Masham refuses to infer truth from elegance. She criticizes it as a hypothesis and as an “inference from ignorance”: because we cannot understand how God could make mind and body interact, we are not entitled to conclude that God must have chosen a non-interactionist system. She also complains that both Leibnizian pre-established harmony and Malebranchean occasionalism risk making the organization of the body useless or superfluous.

The philosophical principle is clear: hypotheses about mind-body relation must not annul the usefulness of creation, embodiment, or organismic structure. A mind-body theory is defective if it preserves metaphysical neatness by making the body’s organization irrelevant to mental life.

10. The soul’s immortality and future existence

Masham’s view of the soul’s immortality is one of the places where her departure from Cartesianism matters most. She does not rest immortality on a proof that the soul is an immaterial unextended substance. In Occasional Thoughts, she says a future life may be reasonably inferred from certain considerations, including the distribution of good and bad fortune in this world and the natural desire for immortality, but certainty comes only through revelation in the Gospel.

She adds that whether human beings subsist after death depends plainly on the good pleasure of their Maker. Rational arguments may induce hope or probability, but they do not give certainty to most people; the certainty of resurrection and future reward and punishment is given by Christ.

This is a major thesis in her philosophy of mind. Personal persistence after death is not secured by a transparent metaphysics of immaterial substance. It is secured by divine will and revelation. That does not demote the soul. It relocates the doctrine of immortality from Cartesian ontology to Christian providence and resurrection.

It also has moral-psychological consequences. Without assurance of a future state, the law of reason may not always appear prudentially decisive to ordinary agents, especially when present pleasure is strong. Revelation unites happiness and duty by making clear that obedience to divine law is not merely noble but finally rational.

11. God’s mind: divine intelligence, will, and moral order

Masham’s account of God’s mind must be taken as a central part of her philosophy of mind. God is not merely a theological postulate attached to a secular psychology. God is the infinite intelligent source of the order within which finite minds know, love, choose, and become happy.

In Occasional Thoughts, Masham argues that from our own existence, the order and beauty of the universe, and the pleasure of life, reason can infer a first cause that is intelligent, wise, powerful, and good. The attributes of God are visible in the works of creation; God himself is an invisible being known through these exemplifications.

Her conception of divine will is especially important. The divine will is not like ours: it is not a series of successive, disconnected, inconsistent determinations. It is one steady, uniform, unchangeable result of infinite wisdom and benevolence extending to all God’s works.

This gives Masham a non-voluntarist account of morality. God’s will does not make right arbitrary. Rather, the divine will expresses wisdom, benevolence, order, and the relations among things. The law of God and the law of reason or nature are therefore integrated. God’s mind is the ultimate ground of intelligibility and normativity, not a capricious external legislator.

At the same time, Masham rejects Malebranchean overreach. We do not know God by directly seeing all things in God, nor are we entitled to say that God alone immediately produces all our sensations in such a way that creatures are mentally and morally dispensable. God’s mind is known through creation, not by erasing creation.

12. Angels, archangels, and the wider intellectual creation

Masham’s remarks on angels and other intelligent beings are not incidental. They perform real philosophical work.

First, they limit human presumption. Against Norris’s claim that heavenly or angelic love should model our earthly love, Masham replies that even if heavenly love were as he says, it would not follow that the same measure applies on earth. We do not know what we will be in heaven, what may be added to or changed in our present faculties, or what the faculties of angels and archangels are. We are even less acquainted with angelic faculties than with our own future state.

This is a comparative philosophy of mind. Human psychology is not angelic psychology. Earthly minds are embodied, social, and dependent on creaturely affection. It may be no more reasonable for human beings to ask to be like angels on earth than for fish to ask to fly like birds. That analogy is not flippant; it expresses an ecological view of mind. Cognitive and affective powers are fitted to a creature’s condition.

Second, angels and other intelligent beings expand the domain of moral agency. Masham says Scripture speaks of ranks of intelligent beings besides ourselves, including beings superior to us who have fallen through disobedience. She also speaks of “orders of thinking beings” whom God has made free agents. This means that freedom, dependence on God, and the possibility of disordered self-sufficiency are not uniquely human. They are features of created thinking beings more generally.

Third, the plurality of intelligent beings enforces epistemic humility. In Occasional Thoughts, Masham says that, given the “numberless worlds” surrounding us, we are only a small part of the intellectual creation, and we may need to know much more of the whole extent of intelligent beings to see the full beauty of providence. This is a strikingly non-anthropocentric move. Human beings are not the whole of mind. They are one order within a broader creation of intelligences.

The angelology therefore belongs inside the philosophy of mind. It gives Masham a hierarchy: God as infinite mind; created superior minds such as angels; human minds as finite, embodied, social, rational agents; and perhaps brutes as beings with sense and appetite but without the full rational use that defines human moral agency. Her theory of mind is not merely human psychology; it is a doctrine of intellectual creation.

13. Brutes, humans, and the boundary of rational agency

Masham says comparatively little about animal minds, but what she does say is revealing. Reason is the faculty that distinguishes us from beasts, and the actual use of reason distinguishes us from madmen. This suggests a layered psychology. Brutes may have sensation, appetite, and perhaps pleasure, but they do not possess the rational faculty required for moral law, reflective assent, and accountable liberty.

Human beings contain an appetitive or animal dimension, but that dimension must be governed by reason. The human mind is therefore not defined by the absence of appetite but by the presence of a higher governing faculty. Masham’s rational creature is not an angel and not a brute: it is a being whose passions can be regulated by reason, whose habits can be formed by education, and whose freedom can be used well or badly.

14. Religion, revelation, and rational mind

Masham’s appeal to revelation can be misunderstood. She does not use revelation to bypass reason. She uses reason to determine when revelation is credible, and then treats revelation as expanding and enforcing what reason alone often fails to secure in practice.

Her view is that the commands of Christ, except for two positive institutions, are also dictates of nature discoverable by the light of reason. But few would actually discern the law of nature in its full extent by natural light alone, and even if they did, present pleasure and bad habits often overwhelm rational compliance. Therefore Christian revelation is universally adapted to making people truly virtuous because it plainly and authoritatively teaches the law of reason as the law of God, supported by rewards and punishments.

This is a practical epistemology of finite minds. The problem is not that reason is worthless. The problem is that human minds are finite, badly educated, passion-prone, socially corrupted, and often unable to sustain rational motivation. Revelation supplies clarity, authority, and motivational force without annulling reason.

15. A systematic extraction: Masham’s philosophy of mind in theses

The following theses capture the reconstructed system.

First, mind is not bare cognition. It includes understanding, reason, love, desire, appetite, habit, assent, volition, and freedom. Masham’s mind is practical and affective from the start.

Second, reason is the governing faculty of rational creatures. It judges evidence, connects ideas through intermediate ideas, distinguishes probability from certainty, governs assent, and discovers the law of nature insofar as finite agents can discover it.

Third, mental life begins from sense, reflection, and lived experience. Masham’s account of love begins from what we find in ourselves: pleasure, affection, desire, and the experience of being pleased by objects. Her natural theology likewise moves from experience of self, world, order, beauty, and pleasure to God.

Fourth, love is a simple act or disposition of mind toward what pleases. Desire and benevolence are not separate species of love but consequent acts that vary with the object. This theory allows creaturely love, neighborly love, friendship, family affection, and love of God to belong to one affective psychology.

Fifth, God is to be loved chiefly, not solely. Love of creatures is not inherently idolatrous. On the contrary, the love of creatures is part of how finite minds come to know and love the Creator.

Sixth, virtue is the active principle of a mind sincerely desiring to conform to the divine law, which is also the law of reason or nature. Virtue is not reputation, custom, gendered honor, or external propriety.

Seventh, passions and appetites must be governed, not annihilated. Human beings are embodied and affective; the moral problem is disordered passion, not passion as such.

Eighth, education is mind-formation. Early habits, impressions, and instruction shape future passions, inclinations, and rational capacities. Women’s education is therefore philosophically central because mothers are primary early formers of minds.

Ninth, freedom of action is essential to moral agency. Created minds can act contrary to God’s will; without that capacity there would be no desert, defect, or self-excellence.

Tenth, the soul/mind is not demonstrably an unextended Cartesian substance. Masham is open to extended substance, extension without solidity, and even the possibility that God may annex thought to solid substance. She rejects both ungrounded metaphysical hypotheses and reductive derivations of thought from matter.

Eleventh, mind-body theory must preserve the meaningfulness of embodiment. Occasionalism and pre-established harmony are suspect because they risk making bodily organization and creaturely mediation superfluous.

Twelfth, immortality is not secured by Cartesian metaphysics but by divine will and revelation. Reason can make future existence probable or hopeful; revelation makes it certain.

Thirteenth, God’s mind is the infinite intelligent source of order, goodness, wisdom, and moral law. Divine will is steady, uniform, and benevolent, not arbitrary.

Fourteenth, angelic and other nonhuman intelligent minds are philosophically significant. They show that human mind is one finite order among many created intelligences, and that free agency, dependence, and possible fallenness extend beyond humanity.

Fifteenth, philosophy of mind must be morally useful. Masham’s constant question is not merely “what hypothesis can be imagined?” but “what can a finite rational creature justifiably believe, and how does that belief affect virtue, happiness, education, freedom, and love?”

16. Interpretive disputes and cautions

The first caution is against over-Lockeanizing Masham. She clearly shares Lockean empiricist commitments: sense and reflection, the evidential governance of assent, suspicion of speculative hypotheses, and openness to thinking matter. But she also retains Cudworthian themes: eternal law, moral rationalism, divine wisdom, created freedom, and a metaphysics of order. The recent French reception explicitly notes that Coste’s translation gives her Discourse a Lockean accent, which is a useful warning against reading that accent back into the whole of Masham’s thought.

The second caution is against calling her simply a materialist. Her openness to God annexing thought to solid substance is real, but it is not reductive materialism. Thought is not mechanically derived from solidity; God’s creative power remains central; and she also allows extension without solidity, which can accommodate spiritual or non-bodily substance. Her view is better understood as anti-Cartesian and anti-unextended-substance, not as eliminative or reductive materialism.

The third caution is against treating theology as external to the philosophy. In Masham, God is not a decorative premise. Divine mind grounds the intelligibility of the world, the unity of moral law, the final union of happiness and duty, and the possibility of future existence. Angels and other intelligent beings are also not marginal. They structure her account of intellectual creation and remind us that human psychology is finite, embodied, and condition-specific.

The fourth caution is that her philosophy is deliberately modest. She often refuses to affirm beyond what evidence, experience, reason, and revelation warrant. That is not a failure of system. It is part of the system. Her metaphysics of mind is governed by epistemic humility.

17. Final characterization

Damaris Masham’s philosophy of mind can be described as a theistic, empiricist, moral-rational, developmental, social, anti-occasionalist theory of finite rational agency. The human mind is an embodied and affective rational agent formed by education, moved by love and pleasure, capable of free action, obligated by the law of reason or nature, and dependent on God for happiness, moral order, and future existence.

Her distinctive contribution is to refuse several false alternatives. She refuses reason without affection, piety without reason, love of God without love of creatures, freedom without moral law, education without women’s intellectual cultivation, mind without body, metaphysics without epistemic humility, and human psychology without the wider context of divine and angelic intelligence.

The resulting philosophy of mind is not merely an appendix to Locke, Cudworth, Malebranche, Norris, Astell, or Leibniz. It is a serious independent synthesis: finite mind is the rational and loving activity of a created being, situated in a body and society, ordered toward God, and intelligible only within a larger creation of minds.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
